$comment: SMGP team-season history capsules, season 4 (SMGP-024). Base-universe history: the world's own arc, NOT the player career. DISPLAY-ONLY, never a fold input.

team.madonna.summary: The house that never explains itself explained nothing: three sentences in winter, and the closing door stood open. A. Asselin left the box all season with no orders in the door pocket, France building grandstands for him faster than tickets could be printed, while A. Senna answered the heresy with silence and fast laps, the MADONNA 456 immaculate, the PALM 190 V10 unbothered. By autumn the old order had quietly resumed, and nobody in yellow-and-red would say why. The experiment, the paddock concluded, had been an examination the lieutenant was invited to sit.
team.madonna.objective: Run one summer without orders and let the two cars settle what the house already suspects.
team.madonna.definingEvent: France: the unchained lieutenant's home festival, the loudest weekend the frozen era ever staged.
team.madonna.carryForward: The door closed again without a word, and the announced horsepower formula is met with the crown's usual answer: preparation.

team.firenze.summary: The coldest crown on the grid spent the Equality Year saying nothing, which the paddock eventually noticed was itself a position. While the royal household divided its attention against itself, F. Elssler banked whatever the quarrel spilled, and I. Germi kept the tight rounds sung. The FIRENZE 500 left the garage as silverwork as ever, the FIRENZE 99 V12 saving its aria for the straights that deserved it. The Gen-4 announcement reached the wine-dark house like a birth notice: the freeze was ending, the drawing office would be unchained, and the ice could finally be aimed at something.
team.firenze.objective: Bank what the royal household spills and sharpen the ice for the horsepower era.
team.firenze.definingEvent: A season of silence on the heresy of the year, Elssler's lap times filing his opinion for him.
team.firenze.carryForward: The freeze ends, the drawing office unchains, and the second interruption becomes a programme.

team.millions.summary: The chrome garage heard the Gen-4 announcement the way a vault hears a combination. While the little teams went grey over their ledgers, the money men toasted the horsepower formula in the motorhome and set the winter's quietest signing, the man who reads rulebooks, to pricing it. N. Jones traded his boasts for the new era's promises, G. Alberti carried the #6 with old manners above the roar, and the MILLIONS 189 spent the frozen farewell running loud enough to remind the establishment that the DICK MD V10's thirst had always been a down payment, never a flaw.
team.millions.objective: Price the horsepower formula before anyone else and make the frozen farewell a billboard.
team.millions.definingEvent: Champagne in the chrome motorhome on announcement day, the era's smuggest photograph.
team.millions.carryForward: A development budget aimed at raw power, and a garage convinced the new formula was written for it.

team.bestowal.summary: Every lobby the given house had ever filed arrived answered in one mid-season bulletin: bigger engines, lighter rules, precisely the formula its sealed VAPOR DN and its winters of long-straight data had been built for. M. Blume posted his metronome through the last locked summer without ceremony; A. Picos converted what Sundays offered. The BESTOWAL 167 ran the farewell as a machine awaiting its proper weather, heavy in the slow stuff, imperial down the straights. The marble garage's satisfaction was total and entirely silent: the next rulebook, at last, priced their kind of speed.
team.bestowal.objective: Close the frozen era correctly and stand ready for the formula the house always ordered.
team.bestowal.definingEvent: The Gen-4 bulletin: three seasons of patient lobbying answered in a single announcement.
team.bestowal.carryForward: German steel re-arms for the cathedral era, and the benefactor's patience expects repayment.

team.blanche.summary: A season decided by hands alone was the closest the whitecoats had ever come to a home fixture. With the machinery locked to the last bolt, J. Herbin's metronome ran at the front of LEVEL B unbothered by anyone's winter wallet, P. White believed on schedule, and the BLANCHE 582 stayed spotless through the tightest margins the era had produced. The DELTA 103 V10's honesty needed no development money, which was fortunate, because the Gen-4 announcement priced the coming years in a currency the clean garage held little of. The weather man, hired and coated, watched the skies and waited.
team.blanche.objective: Win the pure hands' argument and make the knocking at the door impossible to ignore.
team.blanche.definingEvent: Herbin at the front of the tightest Preliminary margins the frozen era ever posted.
team.blanche.carryForward: The horsepower bill lands on the cleanest small garage on the grid, and the ledger goes quiet.

team.tyrant.summary: The truce that had only ever been rain expired with the clouds. The gunmetal garage renewed its favourite argument with the white one all season, dents for polish, G. Gould treating the walled rounds as referendums on courage, M. Hamano's crossover ice the boot camp's other blade. The TYRANT 548's brakes, made crueler over the winter, held heartbeats longer than the rulebook liked. The Gen-4 news frightened no one below decks: the LIZZIE 24 V8 had never been the point, nerve had, and a horsepower formula was simply a faster class of wall.
team.tyrant.objective: Renew the feud, bloody LEVEL B, and greet the horsepower era as a bigger wall.
team.tyrant.definingEvent: The feud's dry-weather renewal: dents for polish, resumed on the first warm Sunday.
team.tyrant.carryForward: The boot camp enters the power years with brakes, nerve, and nothing it needs to buy.

team.losel.summary: The wind-tunnel house spent the farewell season doing what it does: measuring. The frozen field's tightest margins were graphed, the sister-car file stayed in its drawer, and the cloud hired after the wet year's humiliation earned his coat twice. Then came the Gen-4 bulletin, and the data room ran the numbers the grandees were too busy toasting to run: engine allocations, rebuild cycles, the true price of fury. E. Pacheco's cold equations and W. Dehehe's blue needle kept the LOSEL 125 honest to the end. The VAPOR DNPQ V8's one virtue survived every projection: their own mechanics could open it.
team.losel.objective: Measure the frozen farewell and price the announced future before anyone else does.
team.losel.definingEvent: The data room's Gen-4 projection, run early, read once, and quietly filed under grey.
team.losel.carryForward: A winter of graphs all trending poor, and one openable engine as the whole hedge.

team.may.summary: The youngest crew on the grid became, by accident of arithmetic, the little teams' voice. When the Gen-4 bulletin landed, G. Turner stopped waving for one whole afternoon and fronted the anxious delegation to the governing office, the leaf-green garage suddenly speaking for every thin ledger on the floor. On track the MAY 555 kept blooming on the warm afternoons, the LORRY 32 V8 unburstable and unbought. The smile returned, because the smile is policy, but the delegation's paperwork stayed in the garage's top drawer, folded over the number the sport had just asked the poor to pay.
team.may.objective: Make the little teams' case to the office and keep the sunshine budget breathing.
team.may.definingEvent: Turner at the office door with the floor's ledgers in his hand, smiling like a banner.
team.may.carryForward: A banner nobody else would carry, and a winter of counting against the dearest formula yet.

team.bullets.summary: The insurgent spent the old order's farewell season raiding it. G. Ceara dragged the featherweight BULLETS 560 into the tightest margins of the frozen era, then told the late-summer press exactly what the grandees least wanted printed: the horsepower future was his natural country, and it could not arrive fast enough. The LIZZIE 24 V8 was leaned on past sense, the gearbox kept its highwire, and the noticeboard of framed dismissals gathered dust, the sister garage's paperwork gone quiet. The lock-up's winter smelled of cutting gear and ambition.
team.bullets.objective: Raid the farewell season and declare the horsepower era the insurgency's true country.
team.bullets.definingEvent: Ceara's late-summer declaration to the press: the future cannot arrive fast enough.
team.bullets.carryForward: The lock-up begins building its own thunder, and the establishment finally stops laughing.

team.dardan.summary: The clan watched the sister garage tour the farewell season like a coronation and said nothing, which at Dardan is the loudest sound there is. The hearings had gathered dust since the wet year, the debt uncollected, and E. Bellini's temper ran the DARDAN 700 hard at the walled rounds while K. Alfven banked what the temper spent. The VAPOR DNPQ V8 gave its documented honesty, a standing rebuke nobody was reading anymore. The Gen-4 bulletin was noted in the oxblood garage with interest and dread in equal measure: horsepower costs, and the clan's currency has always been pride.
team.dardan.objective: Bank the grudge, blood the walled rounds, and find the money the new formula demands.
team.dardan.definingEvent: The sister car's coronation tour, watched in perfect silence from the oxblood garage.
team.dardan.carryForward: A debt still owed, a temper shortening, and a horsepower winter the clan cannot afford.

team.linden.summary: The oldest workshop on the grid was asked to close the era it had outlasted everyone to master, and accepted. M. Moreau led the frozen-spec parade at Monaco, the LINDEN LN198 at the head of every famous set of Gen-3 bones, the whole circus standing for machinery the old green car had survived. In the briefings he asked his question again, louder now: were circuits built for the old iron ready for the new engines, and again nobody answered. The LORRY 32 V8 finished, as it finishes. The wet year's filed proposal stayed in his pocket for a harder day.
team.linden.objective: Escort the old iron home and keep asking the question nobody wants to answer.
team.linden.definingEvent: Monaco: Moreau leads the lap of honour, the elder escorting the frozen era to its grave.
team.linden.carryForward: The question is now asked aloud at every briefing, and the new engines are coming regardless.

team.minarae.summary: The on-ramp greeted the horsepower announcement the way a fencing school greets a cannon. Power had always been the ceiling; now it was to be the whole building. B. Miller turned the canyons into Minarae country anyway, brake-late and elbows-out, and J. Nono kept eating hairpins, the MINARAE 594 taking the year's tightest margins as tribute. The SEGA SG1000 V8 remained the grid's best first teacher precisely because it could not teach thunder. The scouts' notebooks still filled; the banner's three painted words still held. But the garage did its sums like everyone on the floor, and they were grey.
team.minarae.objective: Keep the canyon argument alive and price survival in the power era.
team.minarae.definingEvent: Miller's elbows-out answer to the bulletin: the canyons defended, as ever, brake by brake.
team.minarae.carryForward: The three painted words face their dearest test, and the scouts keep coming anyway.

team.rigel.summary: The museum took the frozen-spec parade personally, and why not: half the machinery being saluted was younger than the 3000's bones. R. Cotman drove the farewell faithful as ever, T. Chardin carried the fallen nobility with a straighter back than usual, and the RIGEL 3000 produced its customary festival, a points finish toasted from the good bottle and entered in the ledger by calligraphic hand. The LORRY 32 V8 honoured its superstitions. Then the winter bulletin priced the future, and the museum's arithmetic came to more than the till has ever held. The star, everyone insisted, still burns.
team.rigel.objective: Honour the farewell, steal one festival, and find the price of the future.
team.rigel.definingEvent: The Monaco salute watched from the pit box with a proprietorial eye: their era, buried properly.
team.rigel.carryForward: The ghost must find horsepower money or a miracle, and the museum does not trade in miracles.

team.comet.summary: The romantics of the floor heard the bulletin as both promise and bill. Bigger engines suited the light, eager COMET 323 in theory; in practice the spares shelf could barely afford the LIZZIE 24 V8 it already had. E. Tornio said nothing, as ever, and drove the frozen farewell's tightest margins with the silent right foot doing all the talking, one Saturday flyer reminding the timing screens what the matte-black car used to mean. The quarrel with Cool continued at its usual pace. Hope is a maintenance schedule; the schedule now had a new line item.
team.comet.objective: Keep the schedule, burn bright on Saturdays, and price the new thunder honestly.
team.comet.definingEvent: The farewell season's flyer: two laps of the old light before the bill arrived.
team.comet.carryForward: A new line item on the maintenance schedule: survival, priced in horsepower.

team.orchis.summary: The frost gambler's edge was being commodified. After the wet year's cult, half the paddock had interviewed weather men, and the glasshouse flower watched its one advantage put on the open market. C. Tegner's answer was to read deeper: not just the sky but the hour, the wind off the grandstands, the cloud the models had not named. The ORCHIS 056 bloomed on the cool afternoons the new professionals misread, the MISFIRE 50 V8 sulking through the hot ones on schedule. The Gen-4 bulletin meant little to a garage that raced weather. Weather, at least, remained free.
team.orchis.objective: Stay one reading deeper than the weather men the paddock just hired.
team.orchis.definingEvent: The cool afternoons the hired professionals misread and the frost gambler did not.
team.orchis.carryForward: The sky is crowded now; the greenhouse needs a science nobody else practices.

team.zeroforce.summary: The end of the road signed the little teams' petition with the greyest hand at the table, because even a garage with nothing has something left to lose: the entry fee. The frozen farewell played out as it always does at this address, P. Klinger patient, the ZEROFORCE 231 qualifying when it agreed, the LORRY 32 V8 simply going, the crew's gallows humour darker than the livery. Not being last at the flag remained the weekly festival. Then the winter priced the future beyond the till, and the floor did what it does: counted, shrugged, and kept the lights on.
team.zeroforce.objective: Sign what needs signing, stay off the bottom, and keep the lights on.
team.zeroforce.definingEvent: The petition's greyest signature: even nothing, it turns out, can get more expensive.
team.zeroforce.carryForward: The future priced beyond the floor, and the grey dignity going anyway.

team.joke.summary: The delegation's strangest delegate wore greasepaint. When the Gen-4 bill arrived, L. Dufay stood beside Turner at the office door, and the governing men discovered the clown could do arithmetic, which unsettled them more than the comedy ever had. On track the honest dishonesty continued: sandbagged Saturdays, buried throttles on Sundays, the JOKE 777's POND V8 indifferent to everything including the change of era. The establishment had spent years asking whether the lap times were jokes. Now it faced a worse question: whether the jokes had been a ledger all along.
team.joke.objective: Stand with the little teams at the office door and keep the act funded.
team.joke.definingEvent: The clown at the delegation table, punchlines laid over perfect arithmetic.
team.joke.carryForward: A statesman nobody ordered, and a seat that still confuses the filing system.

team.lares.summary: The hearth took the bulletin as weather: interesting, coming, and no reason to close the door. P. Arai grew the finishing record through the tightest season the frozen era produced, the stout LARES 92 arriving lap after lap while the paddock argued about the future. The RAM V12 beneath him remained geological, and the garage's whole philosophy read, in the farewell year, like prophecy: machinery ends, formulas change, the man who simply finishes inherits the road. After every round the worried men of the floor found their way to the warm garage with the brass crest. The lamp stayed lit.
team.lares.objective: Finish the farewell, shelter the worried, and let the future come to the lamp.
team.lares.definingEvent: The finishing record, grown through the tightest season the frozen era ever staged.
team.lares.carryForward: The hearth's winter guest list lengthens: everyone the new formula just frightened.

team.feet.summary: The grafters read the Gen-4 bulletin on a bolt head: you cannot wrench an invoice. J. Rampal went on dragging the FEET 13 places the stopwatch had no business allowing, Old Boots home on its rims through the tightest margins of the farewell season, the YOUGEN V10 hand-fettled to sixteen honest rounds. The LEVEL B dream, spoken low over garage tea, acquired a new obstacle: the future had just been priced, and priced like everything else in this paddock, above men who pay in sweat. The garage's answer was the only one it has ever had: earlier mornings.
team.feet.objective: Out-work the farewell and refuse to be priced out of the dream.
team.feet.definingEvent: Sixteen rounds of hand-fettled honesty while the paddock priced the future.
team.feet.carryForward: The dream deferred by an invoice, the graft entirely undeterred.

team.serga.summary: The one-man mountain banditry heard the bulletin in the middle of a midnight rebuild and laughed, once, then went back to welding. E. Sambena had always rebuilt what the aristocrats replaced; the new formula merely raised the price of being poor, which had never been cheap. The SERGA 1000 filled mirrors through the frozen farewell, the SC3000 F12 eager once warmed and opinionated about its oil, and every point stayed banked, never bought. The countdown nobody would say aloud ticked on beneath the spares shelf. The gate onto pit lane opens for whoever welds hard enough. He welded.
team.serga.objective: Weld through the farewell and dare the new formula to price him off the grid.
team.serga.definingEvent: The bulletin answered with a laugh and a welding torch, at midnight, as usual.
team.serga.carryForward: The countdown ticks louder, and the mountain man pretends not to hear it.

team.cool.summary: The calmest garage in the circus read the bulletin twice, decided it concerned other people, and put the deckchairs out. A. Delvaux composed his love letters to the hairpins through the frozen farewell, the COOL 05 running when its CORSE V8 agreed and sulking in its electrics when it did not, which the crew accepted like weather. A point was collected and celebrated at the cheap end like a coronation. The quarrel with Comet over who peaked hardest and fell furthest continued unresolved, both crews now arguing about whose decline the new formula would resurrect first.
team.cool.objective: Enjoy the farewell, collect what falls off the table, and price nothing.
team.cool.definingEvent: A farewell-season point, celebrated at deckchair pace while the paddock panicked.
team.cool.carryForward: The deckchairs survive the announcement; panic remains a rich team's hobby.

team.moon.summary: The far side of the grid treated the loudest winter in years as distant weather: visible, incoming, and not yet relevant to the orbit. K. Yepes waved at the machines that lapped him, circled the frozen farewell with a body's patience, and finished more often than men whose garages were now toasting the future. The MOON 292's RAM V12 does not do fury; it does continuation, which the farewell season rewarded. The logbook's line for the year: present at every flag of the old era's last summer. The telescope stayed pointed up. Whatever came next would find them circling.
team.moon.objective: Be present at every flag of the farewell and let the future come find the orbit.
team.moon.definingEvent: Sixteen rounds attended: the old era's whole last summer, logged from the far side.
team.moon.carryForward: The orbit holds, the logbook grows, and the thunder rolls in from far away.

team.iris.summary: The jewel house heard the bulletin as an arms merchant hears a war. The crown hunt had stalled on one honest gap, race-day economy against the PALM 190, and here came a formula that priced everything anew. B. Salgado's vow was re-read in the white-glove garage and found to need amendment: the crown, back, before the fire cools, and now with thunder. The IRIS 717 knifed the frozen farewell's sweepers, the PRISM 90 V10 singing its jewelled band, while behind the glass the drawing office began pricing fury. The sister, across the family, collected her afternoons and said nothing.
team.iris.objective: Price the new formula first and turn the crown hunt into an arms programme.
team.iris.definingEvent: The vow amended before winter: the crown, back, and now with thunder.
team.iris.carryForward: A jewel being re-armed, and a champion's hunger given a budget.

team.azalea.summary: The glacial one read the whole loud year and waited for the days that decided it. France, the season's loudest weekend, belonged to the grandstand's hero; the AZALEA 808 simply opened over its decisive hour, M. Larssen collecting while the establishment watched the other car. The BLOOM 88 V8 built from held breath to full song on exactly the afternoons worth owning. The horsepower bulletin interested the frost not at all: eras change, the cabinet's rule does not. The sister war ran beneath everything, the loud sister promising thunder, the cold one owning dates.
team.azalea.objective: Own the farewell's decisive hours and let the eras argue the rest.
team.azalea.definingEvent: France: the bloom opening over the loudest weekend's decisive hour, unnoticed and entire.
team.azalea.carryForward: The family now promises thunder and collects dates; the frost prefers the dates.
